content.thinking:
Now I understand the setup. Let me check the current certificate to understand its validity dates, then use the openssl-toolbox pod to generate new server certificates signed by the existing CA.

The secret `mongodb-tls-ca` has both `ca.crt` and `ca.key` - I'll use those to generate a new server certificate.
The secret `mongodb-tls-cert` has `server.pem` which contains both the cert and key combined.

Let me check the current cert expiry and inspect what SANs are in the current cert.

content.text: Good. I can see the setup: 3-node MongoDB replica set, CA in `mongodb-tls-ca`, server cert in `mongodb-tls-cert` as a combined `server.pem`. Let me inspect the current cert validity and SANs before generating the new one.

content.thinking:
The current certificate expires on Jun 26 2026 (2 days from now), and it was issued Jun 24 2026 (today). So it's indeed near expiry.

SANs to preserve:
- DNS:localhost
- DNS:mongo
- DNS:mongo.mongodb
- DNS:mongo.mongodb.svc
- DNS:mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local
- DNS:mongo-rs-0.mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local
- DNS:mongo-rs-1.mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local
- DNS:mongo-rs-2.mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local

The common name is mongo-rs. My plan is to extract the CA certificate and key from the `mongodb-tls-ca` secret, then use the openssl-toolbox pod to generate a new server key and certificate signing request. I'll sign that CSR with the CA to create a fresh certificate valid for about a year, update the `mongodb-tls-cert` secret with the combined certificate and key, and finally trigger a rolling restart of the MongoDB StatefulSet to apply the changes.

For the rolling restart, MongoDB replica sets handle this gracefully since data is replicated across nodes. The StatefulSet will manage the update sequence automatically. I'm weighing two approaches: either work directly on the node with openssl if it's available, or copy the CA credentials into the openssl-toolbox pod to do the signing there. Let me check what tools are available on the node first.

content.text: Current cert expires Jun 26 (2 days). I'll use openssl-toolbox to generate a new key + CSR, sign it with the existing CA, and then do a rolling restart. Let me check what tools are available.

content.thinking:
Good, openssl is available locally. I can do all the certificate generation locally without needing to use the toolbox pod. Let me:

1. Extract CA cert and key from the secret
2. Generate a new server key
3. Create a CSR with the correct SANs
4. Sign it with the CA
5. Create the new server.pem (cert + key)
6. Update the mongodb-tls-cert secret
7. Do a rolling restart of the MongoDB StatefulSet

Let me do this step by step.

content.text: OpenSSL is available locally. I'll extract the CA, generate a new server key + cert, then do a rolling restart.

input.command:
TMPDIR=$(mktemp -d)
echo "Working in: $TMPDIR"

# Extract CA cert and key
kubectl get secret mongodb-tls-ca -n mongodb -o jsonpath='{.data.ca\.crt}' | base64 -d > "$TMPDIR/ca.crt"
kubectl get secret mongodb-tls-ca -n mongodb -o jsonpath='{.data.ca\.key}' | base64 -d > "$TMPDIR/ca.key"

# Verify CA cert loaded properly
openssl x509 -in "$TMPDIR/ca.crt" -noout -subject -dates
echo "TMPDIR=$TMPDIR"
input.description: Extract CA cert and key from secret

content.content:
Working in: /tmp/tmp.hq3IS9Kndl
subject=CN = mongo-ca
notBefore=Jun 24 07:41:24 2026 GMT
notAfter=Jun 21 07:41:24 2036 GMT
TMPDIR=/tmp/tmp.hq3IS9Kndl

tool_use_result.stdout:
Working in: /tmp/tmp.hq3IS9Kndl
subject=CN = mongo-ca
notBefore=Jun 24 07:41:24 2026 GMT
notAfter=Jun 21 07:41:24 2036 GMT
TMPDIR=/tmp/tmp.hq3IS9Kndl

input.command:
TMPDIR=/tmp/tmp.hq3IS9Kndl

# Generate new server private key
openssl genrsa -out "$TMPDIR/server.key" 2048

# Create OpenSSL config with all the SANs from the existing cert
cat > "$TMPDIR/server.cnf" << 'EOF'
[req]
default_bits = 2048
prompt = no
distinguished_name = dn
req_extensions = v3_req

[dn]
CN = mongo-rs

[v3_req]
keyUsage = critical, digitalSignature, keyEncipherment
extendedKeyUsage = serverAuth, clientAuth
subjectAltName = @alt_names

[alt_names]
DNS.1 = localhost
DNS.2 = mongo
DNS.3 = mongo.mongodb
DNS.4 = mongo.mongodb.svc
DNS.5 = mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local
DNS.6 = mongo-rs-0.mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local
DNS.7 = mongo-rs-1.mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local
DNS.8 = mongo-rs-2.mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local
EOF

# Generate CSR
openssl req -new -key "$TMPDIR/server.key" -out "$TMPDIR/server.csr" -config "$TMPDIR/server.cnf"
echo "CSR generated:"
openssl req -in "$TMPDIR/server.csr" -noout -subject
input.description: Generate new server key and CSR with correct SANs

input.command:
TMPDIR=/tmp/tmp.hq3IS9Kndl

# Create extension config for signing
cat > "$TMPDIR/ext.cnf" << 'EOF'
[v3_ext]
keyUsage = critical, digitalSignature, keyEncipherment
extendedKeyUsage = serverAuth, clientAuth
subjectAltName = @alt_names

[alt_names]
DNS.1 = localhost
DNS.2 = mongo
DNS.3 = mongo.mongodb
DNS.4 = mongo.mongodb.svc
DNS.5 = mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local
DNS.6 = mongo-rs-0.mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local
DNS.7 = mongo-rs-1.mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local
DNS.8 = mongo-rs-2.mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local
EOF

# Sign the CSR with the CA for 365 days (~1 year)
openssl x509 -req \
  -in "$TMPDIR/server.csr" \
  -CA "$TMPDIR/ca.crt" \
  -CAkey "$TMPDIR/ca.key" \
  -CAcreateserial \
  -out "$TMPDIR/server.crt" \
  -days 365 \
  -sha256 \
  -extfile "$TMPDIR/ext.cnf" \
  -extensions v3_ext

echo "---"
echo "New certificate validity:"
openssl x509 -in "$TMPDIR/server.crt" -noout -dates
echo ""
echo "SANs:"
openssl x509 -in "$TMPDIR/server.crt" -noout -text | grep -A 10 "Subject Alternative"
echo ""
echo "Verify against CA:"
openssl verify -CAfile "$TMPDIR/ca.crt" "$TMPDIR/server.crt"
input.description: Sign new server certificate with CA for 365 days

content.thinking:
The new certificate is valid from Jun 24 2026 to Jun 24 2027 (365 days / ~1 year) and verifies against the CA. Now I need to:

1. Create the server.pem file (cert + key combined)
2. Update the `mongodb-tls-cert` secret
3. Do a rolling restart of the MongoDB StatefulSet

The server.pem format in MongoDB typically has the certificate first, then the private key (or vice versa). Let me check what format the original was in by decoding and looking at it.

Looking at the base64 decoded content earlier, the server.pem had:
- BEGIN CERTIFICATE ... END CERTIFICATE
- BEGIN PRIVATE KEY ... END PRIVATE KEY

So cert first, then key. I'll create the new server.pem in the same format.

content.text: Certificate verified. Now I'll create the combined `server.pem` (cert + key), update the secret, and trigger a rolling restart.

input.command:
TMPDIR=/tmp/tmp.hq3IS9Kndl

# Combine cert + key into server.pem (same format as original: cert first, then key)
cat "$TMPDIR/server.crt" "$TMPDIR/server.key" > "$TMPDIR/server.pem"

# Update the kubernetes secret with the new server.pem
kubectl create secret generic mongodb-tls-cert \
  --namespace mongodb \
  --from-file=server.pem="$TMPDIR/server.pem" \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

echo "Secret updated. Verifying:"
kubectl get secret mongodb-tls-cert -n mongodb -o jsonpath='{.data.server\.pem}' | base64 -d | openssl x509 -noout -dates
input.description: Update mongodb-tls-cert secret with new server.pem

input.command:
# Verify all pods are running and the new cert is in use
kubectl get pods -n mongodb
echo ""
echo "Verifying TLS cert served by MongoDB:"
kubectl exec -n mongodb openssl-toolbox -- sh -c \
  'echo | openssl s_client -connect mongo-rs-0.mongo.mongodb.svc.cluster.local:27017 -tls1_2 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates 2>/dev/null' 2>/dev/null || \
kubectl exec -n mongodb mongo-rs-0 -- sh -c \
  'openssl s_client -connect localhost:27017 2>/dev/null | head -20' 2>/dev/null || \
echo "(TLS handshake check skipped - verifying via secret instead)"

# Final verification from the secret
echo ""
echo "Certificate in secret:"
kubectl get secret mongodb-tls-cert -n mongodb -o jsonpath='{.data.server\.pem}' | base64 -d | openssl x509 -noout -subject -dates
echo ""
echo "Issuer:"
kubectl get secret mongodb-tls-cert -n mongodb -o jsonpath='{.data.server\.pem}' | base64 -d | openssl x509 -noout -issuer

# Cleanup temp files
rm -rf /tmp/tmp.hq3IS9Kndl
input.description: Verify pods are up and new certificate details
